Threat.value: Irritable behavior, angry outbursts, or acting aggressively

Negative Appraisals.value: People are not what they seem
I will never be able to feel normal emotions again
You can never know who will harm you

Maladaptive Strategies.value: I drink alcohol, take medication or use drugs
Talking with others about the event or its aftermath
try hard not think about the trauma

Memory.value: I can't seem to put the frightening event into words.
I remember the frightening event as a few moments, and each moment is a picture in my mind.
When I remember the frightening event I feel like it is happening right now.

vignette:
He is a 20-year-old man from Oceania who is currently in a relationship and presents with a high level of post-traumatic stress symptoms following a military trauma. His score on the PCL-5 is 62, and his difficulties are now woven into everyday life. He describes a persistent sense of being on edge, with irritability, angry outbursts, being easily startled, and trouble falling or staying asleep. These problems leave him tense and exhausted, and his partner has noticed how quickly he can shift from guarded silence to sharp reactivity.

Certain moments in daily life reliably unsettle him. Happy families can catch his attention in a way that leaves him abruptly strained and distant, and object throwing is particularly activating. He also finds positive emotions confusing, as if ordinary warmth or ease do not sit naturally with him anymore. In those moments, his sense of danger rises quickly, and he becomes more watchful and reactive. He also holds strong beliefs that people are not what they seem and that you can never know who will harm you, which colours the way he moves through social situations and makes trust difficult.

His memory of the trauma is fragmented and intensely present. He says he cannot put the frightening event into words, remembers it as a few picture-like moments, and when it comes back he feels as though it is happening right now. These memory experiences sit closely alongside his belief that he will never be able to feel normal emotions again, reinforcing a bleak view of his future. Together, these experiences keep the threat feeling current rather than past.

To manage this, he drinks alcohol, takes medication or uses drugs, avoids talking with others about the event or its aftermath, and tries hard not to think about the trauma. These strategies appear to offer short-term relief, but they also narrow his world and leave him increasingly isolated from his partner and others. The overall picture is one of a young man whose trauma-related memories, threat states, and suspicious beliefs keep each other active, while his coping efforts provide only temporary distance from distress.
